data.title: Jefferies Adjusts Price Target on Prudential Financial to $154 From $152, Maintains Buy Rating
data.description: Prudential Financial has an average rating of hold and mean price target of $131.77, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Neinor Homes Commences Collective Redundancy Process
data.description:
Neinor Homes on Friday announced a collective redundancy procedure to boost competitiveness and efficiency.

The Spanish residential developer said the move follows its acquisition in September 2024 of assets related to the property management business conducted on behalf of third parties by Promociones Habitat, which included 118 employees.

data.title: Jefferies Adjusts Price Target on MetLife to $104 From $101, Maintains Buy Rating
data.description: MetLife has an average rating of overweight and mean price target of $96.77, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Lululemon up after Needham upgrades to 'buy' on strong holiday season sales
data.description:
** Shares of athleisure wear maker Lululemon Athletica up ~1.7% at $399.50 premarket

** Brokerage Needham upgrades stock to "buy" from "hold", with a PT of $475

** Says LULU appears to have had a strong holiday season, even as co guided Q4 very conservatively due to the compressed holiday season, helping co outperform its guidance

** Believes co will partially raise guidance at ICR conference next week while leaving room to potentially beat when reporting full Q4 results

** Sees low tariff risk as only 2% of LULU's sales base is made in China and imported to the U.S.

** 20 of 36 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 13 "hold" and three "sell" or lower; with a median PT of $389 - data compiled by LSEG

** LULU fell ~25% last year

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

Thousands of people displaced by the Los Angeles fires this week face the daunting task of finding accommodation — in a region already seeing a housing crunch. And helping to lead the recovery of California's largest city will be one of its biggest critics: President-elect Donald Trump. While investigators have yet to determine a cause for the fires, we found that one L.A. utility didn't implement common wildfire safety measures as dangerous winds picked up.

Today's Headlines

With entire neighborhoods destroyed, a chunk of Los Angeles's population has shifted south and started on a challenging search for new homes.

The L.A. disaster will test the often-tense relationship between Trump and California Gov. Gavin Newsom, and offer an early signal of how the incoming president will govern.

The wildfires and other natural disasters stand to raise home-insurance rates on customers hundreds or even thousands of miles away.

Investors delivered a swift judgment on who has the most to lose from the recent run-up in global interest rates: the U.K.

Saudi Arabia and other Arab states are jockeying for influence in Syria, overlooking the new leaders' jihadist past in hopes of advancing strategic goals.

Trump says the world is free-riding on U.S. health spending. He has a point, write David Wainer and Jon Sindreu.

Live From The Markets

Federal Reserve official Alberto Musalem says further interest-rate cuts can wait.

Read It Here First

An L.A. utility didn't implement common wildfire safety measures.

The Los Angeles Department of Water and Power, one of the utilities delivering electricity to fire-ravaged areas, hasn't developed a plan to proactively shut off parts of its system during windstorms to reduce the risk of sparks from its power lines, Katherine Blunt reports. It's a safety protocol that every other big California power provider has. A spokeswoman for LADWP said it has other safety measures in place. The causes of the L.A. wildfires are under investigation.

Companies are tearing up the policies Trump hates.

America's corporate bosses are reconsidering diversity programs, climate-change efforts and other Trump targets. Alongside the change in the White House, business leaders say the nation's shifting legal landscape and cooling job market are spurring the changes, Erich Schwartzel and Chip Cutter report. Companies seeking Trump's favor have plenty to gain from his promises to cut regulations and taxes — and expect the new president to move quickly.

Expert Take

Q: Trump isn't expected to get jail time in his hush-money trial. Why does sentencing matter?

The president-elect faces a New York sentencing today for covering up hush money paid to an adult-film star. Trump's legal team had sought to spare him the stigma of the hearing days before he returns to the White House, but the Supreme Court on Thursday rejected their arguments. The presiding trial judge made clear, however, that Trump wouldn't be headed to prison. Corinne Ramey spoke to us about what's expected to happen.

A: The court hearing is both unprecedented and largely a formality. The presiding judge, Juan Merchan, has indicated that he doesn't intend to order a prison term and will sentence the president-elect to an unconditional discharge, which carries no punishment at all.

The sentencing will force Trump to revisit his hush-money conviction at a time when he's on the cusp of his inauguration. Merchan will likely address Trump and speak about the conduct that led to the jury's guilty verdict.

"Sentencing has always been a very moralistic event," said John Coffee Jr., a Columbia Law School professor. "It is a verbal tongue lashing."

Prosecutors, Trump's lawyers and the president-elect himself will likely have the chance to address the court as well. In an unusual move, Merchan has said he will allow audio of the sentencing hearing to become public after the proceeding.

The sentencing also finalizes Trump's felony conviction. While most defendants face practical consequences of being a felon, like trouble securing housing or employment, the designation is unlikely to have a significant impact on Trump.

See The Story

See how a fire in the hills turned into a historic blaze.

A brush fire on Tuesday set off one of the most destructive blazes in Los Angeles history: the Palisades fire. By Thursday evening, nearly 20,000 acres of land and thousands of structures had been burned by the fire. Here's how the blaze swiftly traveled from the brush-filled hills to nearby residential enclaves, forcing residents to flee along gridlocked paths.

Happening Today

The Labor Department releases its December jobs report at 8:30 a.m. ET.

TikTok will argue its case before the Supreme Court, which is deciding whether the government can ban the platform if it doesn't sever ties with China.

Trump is set to be sentenced in a New York court for covering up hush money paid to a porn star.

Earnings: Constellation Brands, Delta Air Lines, Walgreens Boots Alliance, Neogen

Number Of The Day:

19.3%

The average tip at full-service restaurants for the three months that ended Sept. 30, the lowest percentage in at least six years, according to Toast, which operates restaurant payment systems. The decline highlights a bind restaurants find themselves in, as they face rising costs of ingredients and labor amid customer frustration over spiraling bills.

And Finally...

Taiwan army veterans won't stop goose-stepping.

Taiwan's new defense minister, Wellington Koo, has called a halt to the army's tradition of goose-stepping, the now-taboo lock-kneed marching style adopted on the Chinese mainland nearly 100 years ago. As Taiwan tries to build up its military, Koo says the foot-stomping marching style is outdated and has no benefit in modern warfare. The army's old guard is kicking back.

data.title: Delta Air Lines Expects Premium Travel to Power Earnings — WSJ
data.description:
By Alison Sider

Delta Air Lines expects a strong appetite for high-end travel to fuel profit in 2025, said Chief Executive Ed Bastian.

The forecast comes as the airline posted better-than-expected revenue growth in the latest quarter as demand picked up. The airline's sales of premium products rose 8% while revenue from sales of main cabin tickets rose 2% in the fourth quarter.

Bastian said that the clarity of the election outcome helped restore travelers' confidence and unleashed more corporate spending. The carrier said quarterly corporate sales rose 10% from a year earlier.

"Overall consumer demand, particularly premium demand, has also been very, very healthy through the holiday season," Bastian said in an interview. "The outlook in the first quarter is quite good."

Delta and United Airlines posted the U.S. airline industry's biggest profits last year with a focus on upscale offerings for more affluent consumers.

  • Delta on Friday reported record fourth-quarter revenue.
  • Profit of $843 million was down 59% from a year earlier, when the airline reported a gain from an investment in private-jet membership company Wheels Up.
  • On an adjusted basis, the Atlanta-based carrier reported earnings of $1.85 a share, beating the $1.76 expected among analysts polled by FactSet.
  • The airline expects adjusted first-quarter profit to be between 70 cents and $1 a share.

A glut of cheap seats weighed on airfares during much of last year, but that changed as carriers-led by discounters-pulled back on money-losing flying. Industry analysts say more restrained supply is now helping to boost airline revenue and bolstering fares.

"That sets an industry backdrop that is really healthy," Bastian said.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Jefferies Financial Fiscal Fourth-Quarter Results Rise Annually Amid Investment Banking Strength
data.description:
Jefferies Financial Group's fiscal fourth-quarter results rose year over year driven by gains across its investment banking and asset management divisions.

The financial services company's earnings came in at $0.93 per share for the quarter ended November, up from $0.29 the year before, it said Wednesday. Four analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $0.97. Net revenue jumped 63% year over year to $1.96 billion, topping three analysts' estimate of $1.83 billion.

The company's quarterly results reflect "strong performance" in investment banking while asset management fee and investment return net revenue were substantially higher from last year, "reflecting fee growth and strong overall performance from a number of strategies," Chief Executive Richard Handler and President Brian Friedman said in a statement.

Investment banking net revenue soared to $986.8 million from $571.8 million in the prior-year quarter. Advisory revenue surged 91% to $596.7 million amid market share gains and increased global mergers and acquisitions activity, according to the company.

Total capital markets revenue climbed to $651.7 million from $486.2 million last year due to stronger performance in equities driven by increased volume and more favorable trading opportunities. Asset management net revenue advanced to $314.8 million from $140.6 million the year before.

"More normalized market conditions and the maturation of our platform are beginning to show our earnings potential, as our core businesses have generated meaningfully improved underlying operating margins," according to Handler and Friedman. "We are optimistic about our ability to continue to further expand operating margins as we continue to grow our core businesses."

Jefferies' board of directors increased the company's quarterly dividend by about 14% to $0.40 per share. The dividend is payable Feb. 27 to shareholders of record on Feb. 14.

data.title: UAE Stocks Close Mixed as Oil Gains on Colder Weather
data.description:
Markets in the United Arab Emirates closed mixed on Friday as oil prices climbed amid expectations of higher fuel demand due to colder temperatures across Europe and North America.

At close of trading, the FTSE ADX General Index rose 0.347%, while the DFM General Index was 0.02% in the red.

Brent crude rose over 2% to $79.073 per barrel, bolstered by colder weather driving demand in the Northern Hemisphere. "In addition, spot Asian LNG is trading at a premium to oil, increasing the risk of substitution. Meanwhile, uncertainty over how hawkish Trump will be with Iran will be providing some support. Asian buyers have already been looking for alternative grades from the Middle East, with broader sanctions against Russia and Iran making this oil flow more difficult," analysts added.

Investors are now turning their focus to the US December jobs report, due later today, with Stifel projecting a gain of 163,000 payrolls and a steady unemployment rate of 4.2%.

"Meanwhile, average hourly earnings are expected to rise 0.3% in December and increase 4.0% over the past 12 months for the third consecutive month. Additionally, the participation rate is expected to remain steady at 62.5% in December," Stifel said in a note.

In corporate news, Aldar Properties (ADX:ALDAR) on Thursday priced its first issuance of hybrid notes worth $1 billion, which also marks biggest conventional hybrid in the Middle East. The stock was 0.91% higher at closing.

data.title: Delta Air Lines Announces December Quarter and Full Year 2024 Financial Results
data.description:
Delta Air Lines has released its financial results for the December quarter and full year 2024, showcasing a record-breaking performance in several key areas. The company reported significant growth in revenue, operating profit, and cash flow, reflecting its strong market position and operational efficiency.

Financial Highlights

Delta Air Lines reported a record full year 2024 revenue of $61.6 billion, with a pre-tax income of $4.7 billion and an operating cash flow of $8 billion. The company also achieved a free cash flow of $3.4 billion. For the December quarter, Delta reported an operating revenue of $15.6 billion and a pre-tax income of $1.2 billion.

Key financial metrics include:

  • December quarter GAAP earnings per share (EPS) of $1.29
  • Full year GAAP EPS of $5.33
  • December quarter non-GAAP EPS of $1.85
  • Full year non-GAAP EPS of $6.16

Business and Operational Highlights

Delta's diversified revenue streams, including premium and loyalty segments, contributed 57% of total revenue in 2024. The company saw a 32% increase in cargo revenue in the December quarter and a 14% growth in American Express remuneration. International passenger revenue grew by 6% year-over-year in the December quarter, with the Transatlantic segment showing the strongest improvement.

Operationally, Delta earned the Cirium Platinum Award for operational excellence for the fourth consecutive year and was named the most on-time airline in North America. The company also expanded its fleet with 38 new aircraft deliveries in 2024 and launched new routes to Brisbane, Amsterdam, London, and Lagos.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Delta made significant strides in debt reduction, with adjusted net debt reduced to $18 billion by the end of the December quarter, down from $21.6 billion at the end of 2023. The company also returned to investment grade at all three credit rating agencies. Delta's strategic initiatives included a new pilot training facility in Salt Lake City and a partnership with the Minnesota SAF Hub to open a new blending facility in 2025.

Management's Perspective

Ed Bastian, Delta’s CEO, highlighted the company's strong performance and the $1.4 billion in profit-sharing payments to employees as a testament to their hard work. He expressed optimism for 2025, expecting strong demand for travel and projecting earnings per share greater than $7.35 and free cash flow of more than $4 billion.

Future Outlook

Delta provided guidance for the March quarter and full year 2025, expecting revenue growth of 7-9% and earnings per share between $0.70 and $1.00 for the March quarter. For the full year 2025, the company forecasts earnings per share greater than $7.35 and free cash flow of more than $4 billion.

SEC Filing: DELTA AIR LINES, INC. [ DAL ] - 8-K - Jan. 10, 2025

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources Brief: Keeps Sector Perform and C$51 Target at National Bank On "Largely In-line Budget"; Bank Says "Asset Integration Is Key for Differentiation"
India stocks, rupee, swaps, call at close
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex closed 0.31% lower at 77,378.91, while the broader NSE Nifty 50 index declined 0.4% to 23,431.5, weighed by worries about corporate earnings, while IT stocks gained after comments from sector-leader TCS indicated early signs of a demand revival.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee dropped 0.1% to a record closing low of 85.9650 per U.S. dollar, pressured by a firm dollar and as regional currencies stayed on the defensive ahead of the keenly awaited U.S. nonfarm payrolls report.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN067934G=CC) was quoted at 100.11 rupees, with the yield little changed at 6.7724%, as investors await major economic data from India and the U.S.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was up over 1 bp at 6.5175%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) rose nearly 3 bps to 6.225%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 6.65% and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 6.75%.

data.title: Hopson Development Logs 963 Million Yuan in December 2024 Contracted Sales
data.description:
Hopson Development recorded contracted sales of about 963 million yuan in December 2024, a Friday Hong Kong bourse filing said.

Of this, the real estate firm's property and decoration contracted sales contributed about 900 million yuan and 63 million yuan, respectively.

For the year 2024, contracted sales and gross floor area reached 16.6 billion yuan and 532,918 square metres, respectively, with the average selling price coming in at roughly 29,844 yuan per square meter.

From January to December 2024, property and decoration contracted sales stood at around 15.9 billion yuan and 724 million yuan, respectively.

Delta Air 4Q Cargo Revenue Grew 32% Vs. Year-Ago >DAL
Delta Air 4Q Capacity Up 5% >DAL
Delta Air 4Q Adjusted Operating Income $1.74B, With Adj. Operating Margin 12.0% >DAL
Delta Air 4Q Adj. Rev $14.44B >DAL
Delta Air 4Q Adj. Operating Cash Flow $1.78B >DAL
Delta Air 4Q Adj. Free Cash Flow $678M >DAL
Delta Air 4Q Adj EPS $1.85 >DAL
Delta Air 2024 Adj. Free Cash Flow $3.4B >DAL
Delta 4Q Revenue Tops Forecast After Demand Picked Up
data.description:
By Owen Tucker-Smith

Delta expects travel demand to remain strong this year, after posting fourth-quarter revenue ahead of its earlier forecast on a recent pickup in leisure and corporate travel.

The airline on Friday reported net income of $843 million, or $1.29 a share, compared with a profit of $2.04 billion, or $3.16 a share, in the same quarter a year ago.

Adjusted for one-time items, Delta said earnings were $1.85 a share, beating the expectations of analysts, who predicted adjusted earnings of $1.76 a share, according to FactSet.

Revenue, excluding refinery sales, rose 5.7%, to $14.44 billion, up 5.7% year over year, beating Wall Street's prediction of $14.2 billion. Delta had earlier forecast revenue growth up to 4%.

The company said demand accelerated throughout the fourth quarter, and a handful of the company's highest revenue days in history occurred in November and December.

Delta said premium ticket revenue rose 8%, outpacing main cabin ticket growth of 2%. Corporate sales rose 10% while revenue from international passengers rose 6%.

Chief Executive Ed Bastian said the company expects strong demand for travel to continue into 2025.

For the current quarter, Delta forecast revenue growth between 7% and 9%, higher than the 5.4% growth expected by analysts. It expects adjusted per-share earnings between 70 cents and $1, while analysts were expecting 77 cents.

For the full-year, Delta forecast adjusted earnings above $7.35 a share. Analysts expected $7.44.

Write to Owen Tucker-Smith at owen.tucker-smith@wsj.com

data.title: Quarterhill Brief: Awarded High-Speed Weigh-In-Motion Prescreening Contract in Belgium
Stocks wobble ahead of US jobs data; bonds in the spotlight
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. stock futures fall after market closure
  • Volatility ebbs ahead of critical U.S. payrolls data
  • Dollar towers near 2-yr peaks, up for 6th straight week

Global stocks were under pressure on Friday ahead of a U.S. jobs report later on that could exacerbate or ease the sell-off in the global bond market, while the pound headed for a fourth daily drop after British debt yields soared to 16-year highs.

Volatility was more subdued in early European trading as traders stuck to their positions ahead of the upcoming employment data after this week's gyrations across markets.

European stocks eased, with the STOXX 600 down 0.1%, as gains in telecoms and basic materials offset losses in more defensive sectors such as utilities and consumer staples.

Nasdaq futures and S&P 500 futures were down 0.1%, indicating a modestly lower start later on Wall Street, where markets closed overnight to mark the funeral of former U.S. President Jimmy Carter.

The closely watched U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 8:30 a.m. U.S. Eastern time (1330 GMT) is forecast to show a rise of 160,000 in jobs in December, while unemployment holds at 4.2%.

Anything stronger could see 10-year Treasury yields spike to 13-month peaks and lift the U.S. dollar in the process.

Analysts at ING believe a result below 150,000 new jobs would be needed to stop Treasury yields from rising further.

"Payrolls, as always, are a pivotal report. But we need to deviate materially from consensus to have an effect this time around," said Padhraic Garvey, regional head of research, Americas, at ING.

"Given the move already in Treasuries, there is some talk that Friday's numbers will need to be strong to continue this momentum, and in that sense there is some vulnerability for a lower yield reaction to a consensus outcome."

In Asia, Japan's Nikkei fell 0.9%, taking its weekly loss to 1.6%, while the MSCI index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) closed 1.2% lower on the week.

The VIX volatility index , a measure of investor nervousness, was flat on the day in European trading, having touched a three-week high earlier this week, when anxiety about the rise in global long-term bond yields peaked.

FED CAUTION

Fed officials Patrick Harker, the president of the Philadelphia Fed, and Kansas City President Jeff Schmid signalled they did not believe the central bank needed to cut rates imminently.

This had little bearing on market pricing, as traders have already only priced in around 43 basis points of U.S. rate cuts for 2025. Concerns about President-elect Donald Trump's potentially inflationary agenda have helped set these expectations and have been at the heart of this week's rise in long-term bond yields.

The benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose 1.7 basis points to 4.6977%, below Wednesday's eight-month peak of 4.73%. Traders are watching the 4.739% mark, as a break above here could trigger a rise to 5%, a level not seen since 2007.

This week's near-10 bp rise in Treasury yields has helped push the dollar to a sixth weekly rise. In sharp contrast, British gilt yields (GB10YT=RR) have risen nearly a quarter of a percentage point to around 4.8%, their highest since 2008, which has weighed on the pound .

The pound fell for a fourth day on Friday, dropping 0.1% to $1.2293, having hit its lowest since November 2023 overnight, as concern has mounted over Britain's finances in light of the sharp increase in government borrowing costs, which outweighed the appeal of higher returns on British assets.

A strong U.S. payrolls report could dent sterling further, according to XTB research director Kathleen Brooks.

"A strong payrolls report could add to the selling pressure on UK bonds and increase fears of a fiscal crisis in the UK. It could also weigh on the pound, which has been one of the weakest performers in the FX market since the start of this year," she said.

In commodities, oil prices rose on Friday, with Brent crude futures up 2.6% to $78.95 a barrel, while European natural gas prices (TRNLTTFMc1), fell 2.9%, set for a near-9% fall this week.

Gold prices headed for a 1.6% weekly rise, trading around $2,680, close to its highest since December.
